tagline: Calculate the period, frequency, and length of a simple pendulum at any gravity
keywords: pendulum period, pendulum calculator, simple harmonic motion, pendulum length

faq.question: What is the period of a 1-meter pendulum?
faq.answer: On Earth (g = 9.81 m/s²), a 1-meter pendulum has a period of about 2.006 seconds. This is close to 2 seconds, which is why grandfather clock pendulums are approximately 1 meter long.

faq.question: Does the mass of the pendulum affect the period?
faq.answer: No. For a simple pendulum, the period depends only on length and gravity — not mass. This was first demonstrated by Galileo. (Air resistance makes heavy bobs slightly more practical.)

faq.question: What is the small angle approximation?
faq.answer: The simple formula T = 2π√(L/g) assumes small swings (under ~15°). For larger amplitudes, the period increases. At 45°, the period is about 4% longer than the small-angle prediction.

formulas: Period T = 2π × √(L / g) (small angle approximation)
Frequency f = 1 / T
Large angle correction: T_corrected = T × (1 + θ²/16 + 11θ⁴/3072 + ...)
1-second pendulum length = g / (4π²) ≈ 0.2485 m

tagline: Calculate buoyant force, displaced volume, and whether an object floats or sinks
keywords: buoyancy calculator, Archimedes principle, buoyant force, float or sink, displacement

faq.question: What is Archimedes' principle?
faq.answer: Any object submerged in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces. If this force exceeds the object's weight, it floats.

faq.question: Why do objects float?
faq.answer: An object floats when its average density is less than the fluid. Ice (917 kg/m³) floats in water (1,000 kg/m³) with about 92% submerged. Steel ships float because their average density (including air inside) is less than water.

faq.question: Why is it easier to float in salt water?
faq.answer: Seawater (1,025 kg/m³) is denser than fresh water (1,000 kg/m³), creating a stronger buoyant force. The Dead Sea (1,240 kg/m³) makes floating even easier due to its extreme salt content.

formulas: Buoyant Force = Fluid Density × Object Volume × g
Object Weight = Mass × g (9.81 m/s²)
Net Force = Buoyant Force - Weight (positive = floats)
% Submerged (floating) = Object Density ÷ Fluid Density × 100

tagline: Calculate the right amount of dye, soda ash, and water for your tie-dye project
keywords: tie dye calculator, dye amount, soda ash, tie dye ratio, fiber reactive dye

faq.question: How much dye do I need per shirt?
faq.answer: A medium-weight t-shirt (6 oz) needs about 0.5-1.5 teaspoons of fiber reactive dye total across all colors for medium saturation. Use more for dark colors, less for pastels.

faq.question: What is soda ash and why do I need it?
faq.answer: Soda ash (sodium carbonate) raises pH to activate fiber reactive dyes, creating a permanent chemical bond with cotton fibers. Without it, colors wash out. Soak items for 15-20 minutes before dyeing.

faq.question: How long should I let tie-dye sit?
faq.answer: Let wrapped items cure for 12-24 hours at room temperature (minimum 70°F). Longer curing = more vibrant colors. Keep items damp during curing by sealing in plastic bags or wrap.

formulas: Total Fabric = Number of Items × Average Weight
Dye per Color = (Total Lbs × Dye Rate) ÷ Number of Colors
Soda Ash = ~1 cup per gallon of soak water
Dye rates: Light 0.5 tsp/lb, Medium 1.5, Dark 3, Black 6 tsp/lb

tagline: Calculate how much macrame cord you need for any project based on design and knot type
keywords: macrame cord length, macrame calculator, cord needed, macrame supplies

faq.question: How much cord do I need for macrame?
faq.answer: General rule: cut cords 3-7× the finished length, depending on knot density. Square knots use 4× length, spiral knots 3×, and dense patterns up to 7×. Always add 10-20% extra to be safe.

faq.question: Why do I fold the cord in half?
faq.answer: Most macrame projects start by folding cords in half and attaching them to a dowel or ring with a lark's head knot. This means each cut piece creates two working strands, so cut pieces at 2× the per-strand length.

faq.question: What size macrame cord should I use?
faq.answer: 3mm cord: delicate jewelry and small projects. 4-5mm: plant hangers and medium wall hangings. 6-8mm: large wall hangings and curtains. Single-strand cord is best for detailed knot work.

formulas: Per Strand = (Finished Length × Knot Multiplier) + (Fringe × 2)
Cut Length = Per Strand × 2 (folded in half for lark's head)
Total Cord = Cut Length × (Number of Cords ÷ 2)
Knot multipliers: Spiral 3×, Square 4×, Dense 5-7×

tagline: Estimate how long homework will take based on assignment type, volume, and difficulty
keywords: homework time, study time calculator, homework estimate, assignment time

faq.question: How much homework should students have?
faq.answer: The '10-minute rule' suggests 10 minutes per grade level per night (1st grade = 10 min, 6th = 60 min, 12th = 120 min). Research shows diminishing returns beyond 2 hours for high school students.

faq.question: How fast does the average student read?
faq.answer: Elementary: 100-150 words/min. Middle school: 150-200. High school: 200-250. College: 200-300. Academic texts are read more slowly than fiction (about 50-70% of normal reading speed).

faq.question: How can students complete homework faster?
faq.answer: Use the Pomodoro technique (25 min focused work + 5 min break), eliminate distractions (phone away), start with the hardest subject, and break large assignments into smaller chunks.

formulas: Reading Time = Pages × Minutes per Page × Difficulty Factor
Math Time = Problems × Minutes per Problem × Difficulty Factor
Writing Time = (Words ÷ 100) × Minutes per 100 Words × Difficulty
Total = Reading + Math + Writing Time

tagline: Compare tutoring costs across different formats and estimate total investment
keywords: tutoring cost, tutor rates, tutoring budget, private tutor cost, online tutoring

faq.question: How much does a private tutor cost?
faq.answer: Average rates: peer tutors $20-35/hr, online platforms $40-60/hr, private in-person $50-80/hr, test prep specialists $80-150/hr, and elite prep consultants $150-300+/hr.

faq.question: How many tutoring sessions are needed to see results?
faq.answer: Most students show measurable improvement after 8-12 sessions (about 1-2 months of regular tutoring). Test prep typically requires 20-40 hours of total instruction for significant score gains.

faq.question: Is online or in-person tutoring better?
faq.answer: Both are effective. In-person is better for younger students and hands-on subjects. Online tutoring offers more flexibility, wider tutor selection, and typically lower cost (20-30% less).

formulas: Cost per Session = Hourly Rate × Session Length (hours)
Weekly Cost = Cost per Session × Sessions per Week
Total Cost = Weekly Cost × Number of Weeks
Total Hours = Sessions/Week × Hours/Session × Weeks

tagline: Calculate the expected temperature inside a greenhouse based on outside conditions and design
keywords: greenhouse temperature, greenhouse heating, temperature gain, greenhouse design

faq.question: How much warmer is it inside a greenhouse?
faq.answer: A closed greenhouse in full sun is typically 20-30°F warmer than outside. On cloudy days, the gain drops to 5-10°F. At night with no sun, a greenhouse provides only 5-10°F of protection from cold.

faq.question: Do greenhouses need heating?
faq.answer: In most climates, yes — at least for winter growing. Greenhouses lose heat rapidly at night. Options include propane heaters, electric heaters, thermal mass (water barrels), and ground-source heating.

faq.question: What temperature should I keep my greenhouse?
faq.answer: Cool-season crops (lettuce, spinach): 40-70°F. Warm-season crops (tomatoes, peppers): 60-85°F. Tropical plants: 65-80°F minimum. Most food plants grow best between 65-75°F.

formulas: Solar Gain ≈ 30°F × Sun Intensity × Glazing Transmission × Ventilation Factor
Inside Temp = Outside Temp + Solar Gain
Heating BTU = Floor Area × Heat Loss Coefficient × Temperature Difference
Night temperatures drop to near outside temps without thermal mass

tagline: Convert wind speeds between mph, km/h, knots, m/s, and Beaufort scale
keywords: wind speed converter, knots to mph, beaufort scale, wind conversion

faq.question: How do you convert knots to mph?
faq.answer: Multiply knots by 1.15078 to get mph. So 20 knots = 23 mph. Knots are used in aviation and marine because 1 knot = 1 nautical mile per hour, which maps cleanly to latitude degrees.

faq.question: What is the Beaufort scale?
faq.answer: The Beaufort scale rates wind force from 0 (calm) to 12 (hurricane force ≥73 mph). Originally created for sailing ships in 1805, it's still used to describe wind conditions: Force 5 (fresh breeze) to Force 10 (storm).

faq.question: What wind speed is dangerous?
faq.answer: 25-30 mph: difficult to walk, small branches break. 40-50 mph: structural damage possible. 60-70 mph: significant damage. 74+ mph: hurricane force — extremely dangerous.

formulas: Beaufort 0=calm, 6=strong breeze(25-31mph), 12=hurricane(73+mph)
Wind force on objects scales with velocity squared

tagline: Convert barometric pressure between units and estimate pressure at altitude
keywords: barometric pressure, pressure converter, inHg to hPa, altitude pressure, weather pressure

faq.question: What is normal barometric pressure?
faq.answer: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inHg (1013.25 hPa/mbar, 760 mmHg, 1 atm, 14.696 psi). Normal range is 29.5-30.5 inHg.

faq.question: How does barometric pressure affect weather?
faq.answer: High pressure (above 30.2 inHg) generally means clear, fair weather. Low pressure (below 29.8 inHg) indicates clouds, rain, or storms. Rapidly falling pressure signals approaching storms.

faq.question: How does altitude affect barometric pressure?
faq.answer: Pressure drops about 1 inHg per 1,000 feet of elevation. At 5,000 feet, pressure is about 24.9 inHg (83% of sea level). Weather stations report 'corrected' pressure adjusted to sea level.

formulas: Pressure at altitude: P = P₀ × (1 - 0.0000225577 × h)^5.25588
h = altitude in meters
~1 inHg decrease per 1,000 feet elevation

tagline: Calculate boat fuel consumption, range, and cost for any trip
keywords: boat fuel consumption, marine fuel calculator, boat MPG, fuel range, boat trip cost

faq.question: How much fuel does a boat use per hour?
faq.answer: It varies enormously: sailboat under power 1-3 GPH, pontoon boat 3-8 GPH, center console 8-20 GPH, cruiser 15-40 GPH, sportfisher 30-80+ GPH. Fuel consumption scales roughly with displacement and speed.

faq.question: What is the 1/3 fuel rule?
faq.answer: The 1/3 rule: use 1/3 of fuel going out, 1/3 returning, and keep 1/3 in reserve for emergencies. This means your usable range is based on only 1/3 of your tank capacity.

faq.question: How do I calculate nautical miles per gallon?
faq.answer: NMPG = Speed (knots) ÷ Fuel Consumption (GPH). For example, 25 knots using 15 GPH = 1.67 NMPG. Boats are far less fuel-efficient than cars, especially at high speeds.

formulas: Trip Duration = Distance ÷ Speed
Fuel Used = Burn Rate × Trip Duration
NMPG = Speed (knots) ÷ Burn Rate (GPH)
Safe Range = (Tank ÷ 3) × NMPG (1/3 fuel rule)

tagline: Calculate volumetric (dimensional) weight for shipping with UPS, FedEx, DHL, and more
keywords: volumetric weight, dimensional weight, DIM weight, shipping weight, package weight

faq.question: What is volumetric (dimensional) weight?
faq.answer: Volumetric weight reflects how much space a package occupies vs its actual weight. Carriers charge based on whichever is greater: actual weight or DIM weight. This prevents light, bulky packages from being shipped cheaply.

faq.question: How do UPS and FedEx calculate DIM weight?
faq.answer: DIM Weight (lbs) = (Length × Width × Height in inches) ÷ 139 for domestic shipments. International shipments use metric: (L × W × H in cm) ÷ 5,000 = kg.

faq.question: How can I avoid DIM weight charges?
faq.answer: Use the smallest box possible, fill void space with air pillows (not peanuts), consider poly mailers for soft goods, and compare carriers — some have more favorable DIM factors.

formulas: DIM Weight (domestic) = (L × W × H in inches) ÷ 139
DIM Weight (international) = (L × W × H in cm) ÷ 5,000 (in kg)
Billable Weight = max(DIM Weight, Actual Weight)
DIM factor of 139 = 1 lb per 139 cubic inches

tagline: Calculate generational cohort, life statistics, and demographic data by birth year
keywords: age demographics, generation calculator, birth year generation, age statistics

faq.question: What year does each generation start and end?
faq.answer: Greatest: before 1928. Silent: 1928-1945. Boomers: 1946-1964. Gen X: 1965-1980. Millennials: 1981-1996. Gen Z: 1997-2012. Gen Alpha: 2013-present. Exact boundaries vary by source.

faq.question: What is the average life expectancy?
faq.answer: Global average: ~73 years. Japan: ~84. Australia/Canada/UK: ~81-83. US: ~77 (lower due to healthcare access, obesity, and violence). Life expectancy has increased ~30 years since 1900.

faq.question: How many people are in my generation?
faq.answer: Baby Boomers: ~76 million (US). Gen X: ~65 million. Millennials: ~72 million. Gen Z: ~68 million. Millennials are currently the largest living generation in the US workforce.

formulas: Age = Current Year - Birth Year
Generation determined by birth year ranges
Remaining Life = Life Expectancy - Current Age
Retirement age: 67 for most current workers

tagline: Calculate pattern piece dimensions with seam allowances for any sewing project
keywords: seam allowance, sewing calculator, pattern size, cutting dimensions, fabric cutting

faq.question: What is the standard seam allowance?
faq.answer: Commercial patterns: 5/8" (15mm). Quilting: 1/4". European patterns: 1 cm. Home sewing: 1/2" to 5/8". Specialty (lingerie, activewear): 1/4" to 3/8". Always check your pattern instructions.

faq.question: Why is 5/8" the standard seam allowance?
faq.answer: 5/8" provides enough fabric for finishing (serging, French seams, flat-fell), allows for fitting adjustments, and is easy to measure on most sewing machine throat plates.

faq.question: Should I add seam allowance to all edges?
faq.answer: Add seam allowance to all edges that will be sewn to another piece. Edges on a fold get no seam allowance on the fold line. Bottom hems usually get a separate, larger hem allowance (1-2").

formulas: Cut Width = Finished Width + (2 × Seam Allowance)
Cut Height = Finished Height + (2 × Seam Allowance) + Hem Allowance
Fabric Length = (ceil(Pieces ÷ Pieces Across)) × Cut Height
Standard seam allowance: 5/8" (commercial), 1/4" (quilting)
